titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Ellery Queen Papers
titlestmt.author: by Theresa Battaglio

publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Monday August 13, 2018 at 21:03
Transformed with yale.addEadidUrl.xsl. Adds @url with handle for finding aid. Overwrites @url if already present.

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

did.abstract: The Ellery Queen Papers document the mystery writing career of Frederic Dannay and Manfred B. Lee, writing as "Ellery Queen." The collection includes correspondence, fan mail, telegrams, advertisements, proposals for ventures, photographs, and ephemera relating to Ellery Queen. The collection does not include drafts of manuscripts, but includes American Weekly newspaper articles, likely written by Lee, with clippings describing authentic murder cases, and fan mail documenting their 1948 radio play "A Question of Color." Some of Manfred B. Lee's personal papers such as correspondence and photographs are also included.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The materials are open for research.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The Ellery Queen Papers is the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Ellery Queen Papers. Yale Collection of American Literature, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: The Ellery Queen Papers contain material formerly listed as Uncat.ZA MS.154.
This collection was processed as part of an internship through Simmons College Graduate School of Library and Information Science in July 2009.

bioghist.head: Ellery Queen
bioghist.p: Ellery Queen is a pseudonym for the writing team of Frederic Dannay (1905-1982) and Manfred B. Lee (1905-1971). Dannay and Lee were cousins who entered a writing contest co-sponsored by McClure's Magazine and Lippincott's publishing house, and formed the joint venture of Ellery Queen. Although they did not win the contest, the cousins were rewarded with a book publication deal. Between 1928 and 1971, "Ellery Queen" wrote radio shows, a monthly magazine, a weekly newspaper series, novels, and television series all centered around the investigator "Ellery Queen." In the beginning of their writing career, Dannay and Lee kept their identities secret, but revealed their authorship in 1936. Although their work tended to be a "whodunit" type of story, the authors did address current issues or problems. Their most controversial work was a radio show called "A Question of Color", which debuted on February 12, 1948, and confronted the issue of racism in America. Dannay and Lee were also the co-writers of the Barnaby Ross mystery novels.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Ellery Queen Papers document the mystery writing career of Ellery Queen, a pseudonym for Frederic Dannay and Manfred B. Lee. The collection includes correspondence, fan mail, telegrams, advertisements, proposals for ventures, photographs, and ephemera relating to Ellery Queen. The collection does not include drafts of manuscripts, but includes American Weekly newspaper articles, likely written by Lee, with clippings describing authentic murder cases. Some of Manfred B. Lee's personal papers such as letters, photographs, and cards are also included in the collection. The collection dates from 1929 to 1972, with the bulk of the material falling between 1946 and 1971.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into three series: I. Correspondence, 1929-1971. II. Writings, 1931-1961. III. Other Papers, 1939-1972.

subject.part: Detective and mystery stories, American -- 20th Century.

corpname.part: Mystery Writers of America ( Pictorial works..)
Baker Street Irregulars (Organization : U.S.) ( Pictorial works..)

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series I is organized into two subseries: General Correspondence and Manfred B. Lee Correspondence.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series I, Correspondence consists of professional correspondence related to Ellery Queen and some personal correspondence of Manfred B. Lee. Much of the correspondence consists of outgoing carbon-copied letters.

The first subseries consists of general correspondence documenting the work of Ellery Queen. Correspondence with Jan Broberg includes a substantive response from "Ellery Queen" to interview questions. General correspondence also includes a folder of fan mail which documents the strong stance that Ellery Queen took against racism with the 1948 radio program "A Question of Color." Insight into Manfred B. Lee's personal opinion about this can be gained in a response letter. Also of interest are Lee's responses to fans in which he gives suggestions about how to start writing a mystery novel.

The second subseries consists of Manfred B. Lee's personal correspondence. A 1947 outgoing letter to Bennett Cerf of Random House, written as a response to an article in SRL, details Lee's feelings towards political and social problems in the Bahamas at the time. Other substantive outgoing correspondence includes a 1961 letter to First Lady Jacqueline Kennedy regarding motherhood, with a response from her social secretary.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: General correspondence is organized alphabetically, with folders for "A Question of Color" fan mail and telegrams filed at the end.

odd.head: General note
odd.p: Includes a Swedish review of an Ellery Queen book

odd.head: General note
odd.p: Includes proof of Face to Face dust jacket

odd.head: General note
odd.p: Outgoing letter with response from Letitia Baldrige.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged alphabetically by last name of murder victim.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series II consists of mystery stories published in American Weekly based upon authentic murder cases. American Weekly articles are often annotated, most likely by Lee, and most are accompanied by clippings documenting the original crimes. The majority of files were labeled with the name of the victim (V) and killer (K), and the original organization has been maintained.

did.unittitle: K. Kirby, Harry A. (James J. Crawford)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series III consists of photographs, awards, and other material documenting the career of Ellery Queen. The photographs include an individual portrait of Frederic Dannay, a portrait of both Ellery Queen writers, the 1951 Mystery Writers of America, the 1946 Sherlock Holmes literary society called the Baker Street Irregulars, and photos of Lee at home with his family. The photographs of his family were taken for the Sunday Republican Magazine, and are undated.

Materials documenting the Ellery Queen radio shows include a copy of a CBS advertisement and a listing of the dates and titles of the broadcasted shows, as well as casting cards. Ephemera includes the Ellery Queen letterhead, business card, envelope, and the Library of Congress catalog cards for Ellery Queen's Challenge to the Reader: an anthology . Materials documenting the Ellery Queen contest consist of a press release by NBC.

odd.head: General note
odd.p: Portrait of Frederic Dannay, photographs of Lee at home with his family

odd.head: General note
odd.p: Photographs of Ellery Queen, the Mystery Writers of America, and the Baker Street Irregulars
